Q1: I tried to be really intentional about my study routine, blocking out about three hours a day across the week. Since I learn best by listening, I'd often record myself reading notes aloud or find audio versions of materials when possible. I'd usually study in the library because I don't have internet at home, so I had to plan my LMS time carefully around when I could be on campus. It took some discipline, but having a routine helped me stay on top of eighteen online courses.
Q2: I aimed for regular times, usually mornings before my extracurriculars and late afternoons, but honestly deadlines did shake things up. When multiple assignments were due close together, I'd have to abandon my normal schedule and just focus on what was most urgent. The stress of those crunch times was intense, but I always tried to get back to my routine afterward because consistency is what keeps me grounded.
Q3: Usually I'd log in to check for new announcements or to watch lecture videos since those are my main learning tool. I'd also go in specifically to participate in discussions because I genuinely enjoy connecting with other students and hearing different perspectives. Sometimes it was just to download materials so I could study them later when I didn't have internet access.
Q4: Yes, definitely. After a few weeks, logging in became automatic—like, every time I got to campus, the first thing I'd do was find a computer and check the LMS. It became part of my daily rhythm, almost like checking messages. Having limited internet at home actually forced me to be more deliberate about it, which weirdly made it stick as a habit.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me because I retain so much more when I'm listening rather than just reading. I also found the discussion boards really valuable—hearing other students explain concepts in their own words helped things click for me. The peer interactions kept me motivated and made me feel less alone in struggling with difficult material.
Q6: I'll admit I sometimes skipped over the supplementary reading PDFs, especially when they were really long and dense. Without internet at home, downloading everything wasn't always practical, and reading on a screen for hours isn't my strongest learning mode anyway. If something wasn't clearly connected to an assignment or exam, I'd often deprioritize it just to manage my time better.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to understanding the course material or doing well on assignments. If I could listen to it or discuss it with peers, even better. I also valued anything that gave me feedback quickly so I knew whether I was on the right track.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't quite afford with my workload and limited resources. Required ones got my attention first, no question. But when I did have time for optional stuff, I actually enjoyed it more because there was no pressure—I was doing it purely because I wanted to learn, not because I had to.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. I downloaded lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone during my commute or while doing chores. I also made voice memos summarizing key concepts and played them back repeatedly. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, using notes I'd written out by hand or audio files I'd saved.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing downloaded materials or discussing concepts with study group friends in person, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd listen to recorded lectures multiple times while walking to campus, really absorbing the content, but the system would only show that I watched it once online. My best studying often happened offline.
Q11: Constantly. I'd access a video or document once to download it, then study it extensively offline over the following days. With no internet at home, this was my survival strategy. The LMS would show one brief access, but I might have engaged with that material for hours afterward.
Q12: There were definitely weeks when stress peaked and everything felt overwhelming—usually when multiple deadlines collided or when I was exhausted from balancing extracurriculars. During those times, I'd go through the motions but not really absorb anything. My resilience isn't the strongest, so bouncing back from those low points took real effort and sometimes a CBT session helped me reset.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd log in and just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars sometimes meant I had to rush through LMS activities just to check them off. Confusion with certain topics also made me avoid those modules until I absolutely had to face them.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things or just too tired to focus. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. Those sessions felt unproductive even though technically I was 'active' on the platform.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments, especially the ones that felt overwhelming or where I wasn't sure how to start. Even knowing they were important, the anxiety of not doing them perfectly made me avoid them until the deadline forced my hand. It's something I'm working on—the CBT sessions have helped me recognize that pattern.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my LMS usage. I'd have steady, moderate activity most of the time, but then spike dramatically right before something was due. It wasn't ideal, but with limited resources and high stress, I had to prioritize what was most urgent. Deadlines gave me structure even when my motivation wavered.
Q17: Feedback was huge for me. When I got positive feedback or saw that my assignment scores were strong, it motivated me to engage more and put in extra effort. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd feel uncertain about whether I was on the right track, which sometimes made me hesitant to move forward with new material.
Q18: The course structure helped a lot when it was clear and predictable—knowing exactly when things were due and what was expected made it easier to plan around my limited internet access. When modules were well-organized with clear audio or video components, I thrived. Less structured sections left me feeling lost.
Q19: Having video content was a lifesaver for my learning style, so courses that relied heavily on those made engagement much easier. What made things harder was when important resources were only available in formats I couldn't easily download or access offline. Also, when discussion deadlines were tight, it was hard to participate meaningfully given my internet situation.
Q20: I think sometimes students might be searching for understanding—clicking around, rewatching videos, reading discussions—but still struggling to produce quality work. Or maybe they're dealing with personal issues that make it hard to follow through even when they're trying. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds a bit like me in some ways. I perform reasonably well because I do a lot of studying offline that doesn't show up in the logs. Someone might look at my login frequency and think I'm not engaged, but I'm actually listening to downloaded lectures repeatedly and discussing material with peers face-to-face. The LMS only captures part of the picture.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active, what resources you click on, and whether you're meeting deadlines. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether you're actually understanding or just going through motions. They definitely can't capture offline studying, verbal discussions with peers, or the mental effort you're putting in.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm genuinely curious about the material and actively working to understand it, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's when I find myself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, or when I'm eager to discuss ideas with classmates. True engagement is mental and emotional investment, not just physical presence on a platform.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much I learn through listening—replaying audio, talking through concepts with friends, or recording my own voice notes. It wouldn't capture the stress I manage or how the CBT sessions help me stay emotionally balanced enough to keep going. And it definitely wouldn't show the extra effort required to succeed with limited resources and no home internet.
